title: Sewing a french seam in upholstery fabric
subtitle: Make a self-enclosed seam that hides the raw edges on medium-weight upholstery cloth
excerpt: How to sew a two-pass french seam in cotton drill, linen, or medium-weight upholstery fabric so that the raw edges are completely enclosed within the seam allowance, giving a clean reversible finish suitable for cushion covers, scatter cushions, and visible panel seams.
sourceNotes: The Complete Guide to Upholstery, Cherry Dobson. Sewing for Dummies, Jan Saunders. Singer Sewing Reference Library.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-pass sewn seam in which the raw edges of both fabric panels are enclosed within the finished seam allowance, visible from neither the front nor the reverse of the fabric. The first pass is sewn wrong sides together outside the seam allowance; the seam is then pressed and turned so the right sides face each other, and the second pass encloses the raw edges within the seam.

The width of fabric between the cut edge of the panel and the stitch line. For a french seam in upholstery fabric, a total seam allowance of 15 mm is standard: the first pass takes 7 mm, the raw edge is trimmed to 3 mm, and the second pass takes the remaining 8 mm to enclose the trimmed edge.

The direction of the warp threads (running the length of the fabric roll). Seams sewn on-grain lie flat and do not twist after washing or cleaning. A seam sewn off-grain in woven upholstery fabric will curve or twist in use as the fabric pulls back toward its warp direction.

content.text: is the neatest way to join upholstery fabric where the back of the work may be visible: scatter cushion covers, bolster covers, and any panel where both faces of the seam will be seen. It requires an accurate total
of 15 mm and two passes through the machine (or by hand). The fabric must be cut on the
: a french seam on off-grain fabric will twist.
Step 1, cut the panels on grain

Lay the fabric on a flat cutting surface and confirm the grain by pulling a single warp thread from one cut end: the thread runs the length of the cloth. Mark the cutting lines parallel to this thread. Use the combination square to confirm the cut edge is perpendicular to the grain at the end of each panel. Cut with the upholstery shears in a single continuous stroke for each edge. Panels with cut edges that lean off-grain cannot be corrected later.
Step 2, first pass: wrong sides together

Place the two fabric panels wrong sides together: the patterned or right-side faces facing outward. Pin or clip the seam at the 7 mm position, which is 7 mm inside the planned finished seam position (not 7 mm from the cut edge of the fabric: the total allowance is 15 mm, so the first-pass line is 8 mm inside the cut edge). Sew along this 7 mm line. Press the seam flat, then press it to one side.

After the first pass, trim the raw edge of the seam allowance to within 3 mm of the stitch line using the upholstery shears. Cut as evenly as possible: if trimmed unevenly, the finished seam will have lumpy texture at the high points where more fabric is enclosed. Both layers must be trimmed to the same 3 mm depth.
Step 4, second pass: right sides together

Fold the fabric so the right sides are now together, enclosing the trimmed seam allowance inside the fold. Press firmly along the folded edge so it lies flat with the trimmed edge inside. Sew the second pass 8 mm from this fold, ensuring the first-pass seam allowance is completely enclosed inside the second-pass line. Snip the thread at both ends and press the finished seam flat to one side.
Step 5, check both sides of the finished seam

Open the fabric and inspect both faces of the seam. On the reverse (now the outside of the seam), the stitching should form a neat row with no raw edges visible. On the inside, the trimmed raw edges should be fully enclosed and not visible through the second-pass stitch line. If a raw thread is visible at the fold edge, the first-pass allowance was trimmed unevenly: press and re-sew the second pass 1 mm further from the fold.

A french seam cannot be used on curved seams in medium or heavyweight fabric: the seam allowance will not fold cleanly around a curve without puckering. On curved joins, a flat-felled seam (where the seam allowance is folded and top-stitched flat) is the correct enclosed-seam alternative. French seams are also unsuitable in fabrics with pile (velvet, chenille): the pile at the fold will crush and remain visible after pressing.

attrs.symptom: Raw threads visible inside the finished seam when the fabric is held up to the light
attrs.cause: Seam allowance trimmed unevenly; second pass not sewn far enough from the fold.
attrs.fix: Unpick the second pass, re-press the fold, and re-sew 1 to 2 mm further from the fold edge. Check both layers are folded evenly before sewing.

attrs.symptom: Seam puckers along its length after pressing
attrs.cause: Fabric tension uneven during sewing; fabric pulled or pushed through the machine rather than guided.
attrs.fix: Re-press with a damp cloth. If puckering remains, the stitch tension on the machine needs adjusting — test on a scrap before attempting any further passes.

attrs.symptom: The finished seam twists when the cover is turned right-side out
attrs.cause: Panels cut off-grain; seam sewn at a slight diagonal to the grain line.
attrs.fix: Confirm grain alignment before cutting and sewing on future pieces. A twisted seam cannot be corrected after sewing — the seam allowance would need to be let out and the piece re-cut on grain.
